context: Job chapter number 16, and I'll be looking at verses 1-5, Job 16, verses 1-5 this evening.
point_summary: Job's reply to Eliphaz in chapter 16 is set against the friends' stated purpose in Job 2:11—to mourn and comfort—a purpose the sermon frames as the backdrop for evaluating what they actually delivered.

context: And we're told in Job chapter number 2, in verse number 11, that he had some friends that heard about his troubles...
point_summary: Job 2:11 establishes the friends' stated twofold purpose in coming to Job—to mourn with him and to comfort him—against which their later words will be measured.

context: Many times we're familiar with that phrase, when Job declares, miserable comforters are ye all, and that's Job 16, in verse number 2.
point_summary: Job's friends came with the stated purpose of mourning and comforting him, yet their efforts were so inadequate that Job declares them "miserable comforters"—a striking failure of their own announced mission.

context: according to what we read in Job 2 and verse number 11
point_summary: Job 2:11 establishes that the friends came with the stated purpose of mourning and comforting Job, making their hurtful words all the more a failure of that expressed intent.

context: how long will you vex my soul and break me in pieces, how? With words.
point_summary: Job's own testimony in 19:2 confirms what his friends' words actually accomplished—not comfort, but vexation and being broken in pieces—illustrating the destructive power of ill-spoken words even from well-intentioned visitors.

context: for Job, it was not necessarily a corrective thing that was going on in Job 1. It was a pruning thing.
point_summary: Job 1 is cited to distinguish what was happening to Job — not divine correction for sin, but a proving and pruning work — contrasting with the friends' misapplied sowing-and-reaping theology.

context: I consider his response to Bildad in chapter 19
point_summary: Job's response to Bildad in chapter 19 is cited alongside his response to Eliphaz as evidence of the real-world impact words carry, framing the broader point about intentional, strengthening speech.

context: here in chapter number 16, Job is answering Eliphaz
point_summary: Job's stated intent — to strengthen rather than wound with words — is held up as the model for speech that edifies, directly paralleling the New Testament command against corrupt communication in Ephesians 4:29.

context: if you go back to Job chapter number four
point_summary: Job 4 is cited to show that Job's stated intent to strengthen others with his words was not merely hypothetical — it reflected his actual prior conduct toward Eliphaz.

context: then Eliphaz the Temanite answered and said
point_summary: Job 4:1 introduces Eliphaz's first speech, which the sermon uses to show that Eliphaz himself testified that Job had previously practiced the very ministry of strengthening others that Job now claims he would offer.

context: if we say to commune with thee, wilt thou be grieved? But who can withhold himself from speaking?
point_summary: Eliphaz's own testimony in Job 4:2–3 confirms that Job had already practiced what he claimed in chapter 16 he would do—strengthening and instructing others with his words.

context: Behold, thou hast instructed many, and thou hast strengthened the weak hands.
point_summary: Eliphaz's own testimony in Job 4:3 confirms that Job's declared intent in chapter 16—to strengthen others with his words—was not mere boasting but his established, recognized practice.

context: Thy words have upholden him that was falling, and thou hast strengthened the feeble knees.
point_summary: Job 4:4 is cited as Eliphaz's own testimony that Job had previously done in practice what he now claims in chapter 16 he would do—strengthen others with his words—validating Job's stated intent.

context: I would strengthen you with my mouth, and the moving of my lips should assuage your grief.
point_summary: Job's stated intent in 16:5 — to strengthen and assuage rather than condemn — is validated by Eliphaz's own earlier testimony in Job 4:3–4 that Job had already done exactly that for others.

context: behold, thou hast instructed many, and thou hast strengthened the weak hands.
point_summary: Eliphaz's own testimony about Job—that he had instructed many and strengthened weak hands—confirms the very ministry of words Job claims he would offer in Job 16:5.

context: I would strengthen you with my mouth.
point_summary: Job's own stated intention in 16:5 — to strengthen with his mouth — is validated by Eliphaz's earlier testimony that Job had already done exactly this, strengthening weak hands through his words.

context: Job, you have instructed many, but also you have strengthened the weak hands.
point_summary: Eliphaz's testimony that Job had "instructed many" and "strengthened the weak hands" establishes Job's prior reputation as one who used his words to build up others — the very ministry he now claims he would offer his comforters.

context: Thy words have upholden him that was falling, and thou hast strengthened the feeble knees.
point_summary: Job's words had previously upheld the falling and strengthened feeble knees, establishing that his mouth had been a proven instrument of strengthening others before his own trial came.

context: But now it has come upon thee, and thou faintest, it touches thee, and thou art troubled.
point_summary: Eliphaz's rebuke in verse 5 exposes the contradiction that Job, once known for strengthening others with his words, now faints when affliction touches him personally.

context: And we find, even as we mentioned in our text in Job 16,
point_summary: Eliphaz's own testimony of Job—that he had instructed many and strengthened weak hands with his words—stands in ironic contrast to the miserable comfort Job's friends actually delivered.

context: according to what we read in Job chapter 19, that they were vexing his soul and breaking in pieces his bones with their words.
point_summary: Job's comforters are condemned by their own standard: the very words they once praised Job for using to strengthen others, they wielded instead to vex his soul and break him in pieces.

context: See, there's a great contrast in Job 16.
point_summary: Job 16 presents a sharp contrast between words that strengthen and comfort the suffering and words that are vain, hurtful, and destructive, pressing the question of which pattern marks our own speech.

context: according to Job 2 and verse number 11, I have not read that verse too, I've referenced it a few times, but if you want to look at it, just so you can see what it says, now, when Job's three friends heard of all this evil that was come upon him, they came, everyone from his own place, Eliphaz the Tammanite, and Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Namathite, for they had made an appointment together, notice, to come to mourn with him and to comfort him.
point_summary: Job 2:11 establishes that the three friends came with the stated intention to mourn and comfort Job, yet failed to fulfill that purpose—making them the negative example against which believers should measure their own speech.

context: So if we were to evaluate our words, our speech this evening, would we align more with Job, as it says in chapter 4, instructing many, strengthening the weak hands, thy words have upholden him that was falling,
point_summary: Job 4 is cited as a positive portrait of Job's former speech—instructing many, strengthening weak hands, upholding the falling—against which the congregation is invited to measure their own use of words.

context: Eliphaz specifically being spoken to in chapter 16
point_summary: Job 16's contrast between the friends' failed comfort and Job's model of upholding speech serves as a mirror for evaluating whether our own words strengthen the weak or wound like Eliphaz's vain, hurtful words.

context: Bildad specifically being spoken to in chapter 19
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: according to what Eliphaz says of him in Job 4
point_summary: Eliphaz's testimony about Job in Job 4 confirms that strengthening the weak with his words was not merely Job's stated intention but his established, practiced character.

context: Job 1 and verse number 21
point_summary: Job's declaration that he came naked from the womb and will return naked frames the biblical perspective on contentment: everything possessed in life is a gift from God, not a personal achievement to cling to.

context: naked came I out of my mother's womb and naked shall I return thither. The Lord gave, and the Lord hath taken away. Blessed be the name of the Lord.
point_summary: Job's declaration that "the Lord gave and the Lord hath taken away" models the biblical perspective on contentment—recognizing God as the source of all wealth and responding with worship rather than grief when it is removed.

context: then Job arose, rent his mantle and shaved his head and fell down upon the ground and worshipped.
point_summary: Job's act of worship amid total loss (v. 20) frames his confession in v. 21 that God is the giver of all wealth, establishing the biblical perspective on contentment that possessions are received from God, not owned by man.

context: Job said miserable comforters are ye all
point_summary: Hollow comfort offered by those who know the truth but conceal it mirrors Job's rebuke of comforters whose words bring no genuine relief.

context: The Bible says of Job that man is born a few days and full of troubles
point_summary: Human life is characterized by brevity and trouble, a reality the sermon uses to frame the recurring difficulties Isaac faced in Genesis 26 and the believer's ongoing need for God's faithfulness.

context: Man that's born of woman is few days and full of trouble
point_summary: Job 14:1's phrase "man that is born of woman is few days and full of trouble" is cited to establish that hardship is a universal human reality, framing the Israelites' wilderness trials as part of that broader experience.

reference_text: man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble
context: because man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble. We have difficulties.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Job said this, I have esteemed the words of his mouth more than my necessary food, in Job 23, in verse number 10.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Go over to Job 4 if you will.
point_summary: Job's reputation as one who "instructed many" and "strengthened the weak hands" illustrates that God uses individuals to encourage and uphold others in their weakness.

context: You read there in Job 1 and you read of all the things that befell him
point_summary: Job 1–2 is cited as backdrop to establish the severity of Job's suffering, against which his prior reputation as one who strengthened and encouraged others becomes all the more striking.

context: you read into chapter 2 and see how that it continues to just increase
point_summary: Job 2 is cited as context for the friends who came to comfort Job, whose later failure as "miserable comforters" contrasts with Job's own prior reputation for strengthening and encouraging others.

context: Behold, thou hast instructed many, and thou hast strengthened the weak hands.
point_summary: Job himself, once a strengthener of weak hands and upholder of the falling, later needed that same encouragement—illustrating that every believer may occupy both roles across a lifetime.

context: Thy words have upheld in him that was falling, and thou hast strengthened the feeble knees.
point_summary: Job 4:4 is cited to show that Job, before his own suffering, was a man God used to uphold and strengthen others—illustrating how encouragers can themselves become those who need encouragement.

context: if you read the opening book or the opening chapter of the book of Job
point_summary: Job's opening chapter illustrates that Satan operates under divine permission and limitation, underscoring the believer's security in God's hand from salvation to glorification.

context: 'How many are mine iniquities and sins?' Job says. 'Well, make me to know my transgression and my sin.'
point_summary: Job's plea to know his own transgressions is marshaled as a biblical warrant for the self-examination commanded before taking the Lord's Supper.

reference_text: Job mentions about esteeming the words of God more than is necessary food
context: We find that Job mentions about esteeming the words of God more than is necessary food.
point_summary: Job's words about esteeming God's word more than necessary food illustrate that Scripture is the essential nourishment by which believers are strengthened to remain faithful through trials.

context: ...Job tells us this in Job 14 in verse number one. It's a verse of Scripture that we've often referenced...
point_summary: Job 14:1 is cited to confirm that human life is inherently brief and filled with trouble, grounding David's overwhelming opposition in the universal reality of suffering rather than isolated despair.

context: In fact, Job chapter 14 speaks of the man
point_summary: Human existence is marked by brevity and trouble, as Job 14 confirms, establishing the reality of hardship that frames Paul's own sufferings in 2 Corinthians 4.

context: In Job 42, Job answered the Lord. In verse number one, the Bible tells us that Job is speaking here and answering the Lord.
point_summary: Job's confession that "no thought can be withholden" from God is cited as biblical evidence that God's omniscience extends even to human thoughts, a truth illustrated further by Christ knowing men's thoughts in the Gospels.

context: He says, I know that thou canst do everything, and that no thought can be withholden from thee.
point_summary: Job 42:2's declaration that "no thought can be withheld" from God is cited as biblical proof of divine omniscience—that God knows all things, including human thoughts, before they are spoken.

context: I know that thou canst do everything, and that no thought can be withholden from thee.
point_summary: Job's declaration that God can do "everything" and that "no thought can be withheld" from Him is read as a twofold testimony to divine omnipotence and omniscience, the latter confirmed by Christ's knowledge of unspoken thoughts in the Gospels.

context: Job is replying to one of those that have been referenced as being his friends and of course they're miserable comforters but he says in verse number four of Job 9 he speaking of God is wise in heart and mighty in strength who hath hardened himself against him and hath prospered
point_summary: Job 9:4's pairing of wisdom and strength is read as scriptural warrant that God's omniscience and omnipotence are inseparable—he not only knows what must be done but possesses all power to accomplish it.

context: then the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind and said
point_summary: God's interrogation of Job from the whirlwind — "Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth?" — is cited to demonstrate that Job, like Moses and David, held a clear understanding of God as Creator.

context: Who is this that darkeneth counsel by words without knowledge?
point_summary: God's interrogation of Job from the whirlwind establishes His identity as Creator, demonstrating that Job, like Moses and David, possessed a foundational understanding of God as the maker of all things.

context: Gird up now thy loins like a man, for I will demand of thee and answer thou me.
point_summary: God's interrogation of Job from the whirlwind establishes His identity as Creator, demonstrating that Job, like Moses and David, held a foundational understanding of God as the maker of all things.

context: Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth? Declare, if thou hast understanding.
point_summary: God's interrogation of Job from the whirlwind — "Where were you when I laid the foundations of the earth?" — is cited to show that Job, like Moses and David, possessed a clear understanding of God as Creator.

context: Job chapter number 40 and read about a behemoth, and we think about behemoth, it begins here in Job chapter number 40, and I want you to begin looking with me at verse number 15.
point_summary: God's declaration in Job 40:15 that He made the behemoth "with thee" — alongside Job — is read as evidence that humans and this creature coexisted, directly countering the claim that dinosaurs preceded mankind by 65 million years.

context: the Lord answered Job, if you look at verse number six, it said, then answered the Lord unto Job out of the whirlwind and said
point_summary: Job 40:6 is cited only to establish narrative context — confirming that the voice describing Behemoth in verses 15ff is God himself speaking to Job from the whirlwind.

context: Behold now, behemoth, which I have made with thee, he eateth grass as an ox.
point_summary: God's description of behemoth as a creature he made alongside Job — one with cedar-like tail, iron-like bones, and unrivaled power — is presented as a biblical portrait of a dinosaur coexisting with humans.

context: Lo, now, verse number 16, his strength is in his loins and his force is in the navel of his belly.
point_summary: Job 40:16's description of the behemoth's immense physical strength is cited as part of a cumulative portrait identifying this creature as a large, powerful animal consistent with a dinosaur that coexisted with humans.

context: Verse 17, he moveth his tail like a cedar. The sinews of his stones are wrapped together.
point_summary: Job 40:17's description of the behemoth moving its tail "like a cedar" is cited as physical evidence identifying this creature as a large-tailed dinosaur coexisting with humans.

context: His bones are as strong pieces of brass. His bones are like bars of iron.
point_summary: Job 40:18's description of bones "as strong pieces of brass" and "bars of iron" is read as part of the behemoth's physical portrait, identifying this creature as a massive dinosaur that coexisted with Job and mankind.

context: Notice verse 19, he is the chief of the ways of God. He that made him, who's that? That's God.
point_summary: God's designation of behemoth as "the chief of the ways of God" is read as identifying this creature as the greatest of His earthly creations, consistent with a dinosaur-scale animal made alongside mankind.

context: Surely the mountains bring him forth food, where all the beasts of the field play.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He lieth under the shady trees and the covert of the reed and fens.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The shady trees cover him with their shadow. The willows of the
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The description that God gives here at the end of Job chapter number 40
point_summary: The physical description of behemoth in Job 40—particularly its cedar-like tail—rules out hippopotamus or elephant identifications and points instead to a large sauropod dinosaur such as a brachiosaurus.

context: this here text tells us in verse number 15 about behemoth that he eateth grass as an ox
point_summary: The description of Behemoth eating grass like an ox in Job 40:15 is argued to match fossil evidence of grass-eating sauropods, undermining the evolutionary timeline and supporting identification of Behemoth as a dinosaur rather than a hippopotamus.

context: as we mentioned earlier in Job chapter number 40 about behemoth
point_summary: The biblical descriptions of behemoth (Job 40), Leviathan, and dragons throughout Scripture are presented as scriptural evidence that dinosaurs existed and coexisted with humanity.

reference_text: Man that is born of woman has a few days and a whole lot of trouble
context: Man that is born of woman has a few days and a whole lot of trouble, amen?
point_summary: Human experience guarantees trouble—Job 14:1 is invoked to establish that suffering is universal—but peace is located not in trouble's absence, but in God's presence.

context: Man that is born of a woman is of few days, and full of trouble.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Job said it this way in Job 23. Verse number 10, but he knoweth the way that I take. When he hath tried me, I shall come forth as gold.
point_summary: Trials are God's refining process: just as Job declared he would "come forth as gold," suffering serves the divine purpose of developing believers spiritually for his glory.

context: he knoweth the way that I take. When he hath tried me, I shall come forth as gold.
point_summary: Trials are the refining process through which God produces gold-like spiritual maturity, as Job's unparalleled suffering demonstrates that divine testing yields a proven, purified faith.

context: Job is responding to one of his quote-unquote friends. And Job says this at the first part of Job 9, in verse number 4, speaking of God. He is wise in heart and mighty in strength.
point_summary: Job 9:4's pairing of divine wisdom and divine strength establishes that God is simultaneously omniscient and omnipotent—never powerful without direction, never knowing without ability to act.

context: I want you to read a verse with me in the book of Job. In Job chapter number 9.
point_summary: Creation's precise, life-sustaining order—earth's orbital consistency and daily rotation—leads to Job 9 as Job's own testimony to God's greatness in power and wisdom.

context: You read Job chapter number one. Wow. Wow. As one servant is speaking, telling about a calamity, another one's coming to tell about the next thing
point_summary: Job's cascading calamities in chapter 1 are invoked as the backdrop for understanding the refining purpose of trials, illustrating that God's testing, however overwhelming, produces faith that comes forth as gold.

context: but notice what he says as he's replying to Eliphaz in Job 23 10, he says this, but you know it the way that I take, when he hath tried me, I shall come forth as gold.
point_summary: Job's own testimony amid catastrophic loss affirms that divine trial is purposeful refinement, with the sufferer emerging as gold rather than destroyed.

context: but he knoweth the way that I take. When he hath tried me, I shall come forth as gold.
point_summary: Trials endured in faith produce Christlike character, as Job's suffering illustrates the truth that God's refining work brings his people forth as gold.

context: the Bible tells us in verse number 12 of Job 23, that he says, I have esteemed the words of His mouth more than my necessary food.
point_summary: Job's testimony that he esteemed God's words more than necessary food illustrates the kind of high regard for Scripture that Ezra likewise demonstrated in preparing his heart to seek the law of the Lord.

context: Job says this in Job chapter 14, a verse that we're familiar with, man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble.
point_summary: Human experience universally bears out Job's declaration that life is "full of trouble," and Joseph's story is offered as proof that such troubles are no excuse to abandon obedience to God.

context: But I know that man who is born a woman is a few days and full of trouble.
point_summary: Human frailty and trouble-filled days, drawn from Job 14:1, ground the universal call to cast every care upon a God whose provision for Elijah demonstrates he is able to sustain all his people.

context: Here's what Job said. He said this in Job 23, in verse number 12. Neither have I gone back from the commandment of his lips.
point_summary: God's Word is to be esteemed — treasured and set at a higher value — than the food necessary to sustain physical life, as Job's own testimony declares.

context: I have esteemed the words of his mouth more than my necessary food.
point_summary: Job's declaration that he treasured God's words more than necessary food illustrates the supreme value Scripture deserves, surpassing even physical sustenance.

context: One dieth in his full strength, being holy at ease and quiet. His breasts are full of milk, and his bones are moistened with marrow.
point_summary: Death is the universal equalizer: whether one dies in full strength and ease or in bitterness and deprivation, all alike lie down in the dust covered by worms.

context: His breasts are full of milk, and his bones are moistened with marrow.
point_summary: Job 21:24, read alongside verse 23, illustrates that regardless of the circumstances of one's life—whether full strength or bitterness—all men alike lie down in the dust.

context: And another dieth in the bitterness of his soul, and never eateth with pleasure.
point_summary: Death is the universal equalizer — whether one dies in full strength and ease or in bitterness and sorrow, all alike lie down in the dust.

context: They shall lie down alike in the dust, and the worms shall cover them.
point_summary: Death is the universal equalizer — whether one dies in full strength or in bitterness of soul, Job observes that all alike lie down in the dust covered by worms.

context: man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble
point_summary: Human life is marked by trouble from birth, and this reality — not a sanitized version — is the honest backdrop against which Paul's declaration of pleasure in infirmities must be understood.

context: when I'm tried I should come forth as gold
point_summary: Job's declaration "when I am tried I shall come forth as gold" is cited as an Old Testament example of suffering yielding a God-wrought result, parallel to the early church's rejoicing under trial.

context: Job said it was more necessary to him than his food
point_summary: Job 23:12 is invoked to show that Scripture must be more necessary to the believer than daily food, making regular personal Bible reading a spiritual survival need, not merely a church-attendance supplement.

context: Man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble, according to Job
point_summary: Job 14:1 is cited briefly to affirm that trials are an inevitable part of human life, grounding the call to faith-filled obedience in the certainty that trouble will come.

context: miserable comforters
point_summary: Job's friends are cited as negative examples of mercy-ministry, illustrating that unhelpful words during suffering earn the rebuke "miserable comforters," and that silent presence can serve the grieving better than speech.

context: Man that is born of a woman is a few days and full of trouble
point_summary: Human existence is universally marked by trouble, as Job 14:1 establishes that man born of a woman lives but a few days and is full of it — defined here as distress, affliction, adversity, and tribulation.

context: just read Job one
point_summary: Job 1:1 is invoked as a perspective-giving example of extreme suffering, with the point that reading Job's trials reframes one's own hardships and illustrates the "patience" (steadfast endurance) James commends.

context: hast not thou made an hedge about him and about his house and all that he hath on every side
point_summary: Even Satan's own words in Job 1:10 acknowledge that it is God alone who hedges, blesses, and increases a man's substance — confirming that all material prosperity originates with God.

context: thou hast blessed the work of his hands and his substance is increased in the land
point_summary: Even Satan's accusation in Job 1:10 concedes that it is God alone who blesses a man's work and increases his substance, confirming that all material prosperity originates with God.

context: You read the book of Job and the devil had to get permission, amen? He had to get permission to go after Job.
point_summary: Satan's power over God's people is bounded by divine permission, as illustrated by God's limiting directives to the devil concerning Job.

context: Man that is born of woman is few days and full of trouble
point_summary: Human frailty and trouble are assumed realities of life, making the call to praise God continually—even amid difficulties—all the more striking by contrast.

context: Have you ever read the book of Job?
point_summary: Job's relentless succession of bad news serves as the benchmark for worst-case circumstances, against which God remains worthy of praise regardless of the difficulty.

context: But man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble
point_summary: Job 14:1's description of human life as "few days and full of trouble" grounds the call to offer continual praise as a *sacrifice*—costly precisely because earthly circumstances are not "continually glorious."

context: Job mentions that he knows that his redeemer liveth
point_summary: Job's declaration that his Redeemer lives is cited as evidence that even in the Old Testament, God alone was understood to be the Redeemer—a truth the sermon connects to Ephesians 1's completed plan of redemption.

context: I will set no wicked thing before mine eyes
point_summary: Self-examination before God—who sees every thought, word, and action—is the standard Job models when he covenants to guard what his eyes fix upon.

context: man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble according to what Job tells us
point_summary: Human life is universally marked by brevity and trouble, a reality Job's words confirm and which frames why David—and believers today—are driven to seek the Lord amid trials.

context: Turn over to the book of Job in Job 38
point_summary: God's first direct speech to Job, recorded in Job 38:1, is introduced here as scriptural evidence that God himself addresses creation's phenomena, providing a biblical anchor for the sermon's exploration of the ice age.

context: then the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind and said
point_summary: Job 38:1 introduces God's first speech to Job, cited here as the gateway to verses 22 and 29, where God's own words about snow, hail, ice, and frozen waters are offered as biblical evidence worth considering alongside scientific discussion of the Ice Age.

context: hast thou entered into the treasures of the snow or hast thou seen the treasures of the hail which I have reserved against the time of trouble against the day of battle and war
point_summary: God's rhetorical question to Job about the "treasures of the snow" is presented as biblical testimony worth considering alongside scientific evidence when examining the Ice Age, even though Job himself lived outside the glaciated regions.

context: out of whose womb came the ice and the orifrost of heaven, who hath gendered it
point_summary: God's rhetorical questions to Job about the origin of ice and frost invite consideration of the Ice Age as a phenomenon within the scope of divine creation and biblical history.

context: The waters are hid as with a stone and the face of the deep is frozen
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Over in Job chapter number 38 the Bible would give us some indication in that passage of scripture
point_summary: Job 38:6–7 is read as evidence that angels, including Lucifer as an anointed cherub, were already in existence and joined in praise at the moment God created the earth.

context: whereupon are the foundations thereof fastened or who laid the cornerstone thereof
point_summary: Job 38:6–7 is read as evidence that the angelic host, including Lucifer, were present and praising at the creation of the earth, establishing their existence prior to that creative act.

context: when the morning stars sang together and all the sons of God shouted for joy
point_summary: Job 38:7's reference to the "sons of God" shouting for joy at creation is taken as biblical evidence that angels, including Lucifer, were created and present during creation week.

context: at the end of the book of Job, in Job chapter number 38, there's a conversation that takes place
point_summary: God's direct speech in Job 38 is introduced as the climactic moment when, after human speculation runs its course, the Creator Himself speaks into the dialogue.

context: then the Lord answered Job out of the whirlwind and said, who is this that darkeneth counsel by words without knowledge?
point_summary: God's direct speech from the whirlwind in Job 38 is read here as His sovereign assertion of creatorship, confronting Job's limited knowledge with the unanswerable question of where he was when the earth's foundations were laid.

context: where wast thou, look at this, when I laid the foundations of the earth?
point_summary: God's rhetorical challenge to Job — "Where wast thou when I laid the foundations of the earth?" — establishes that creation preceded humanity entirely, affirming God alone as its architect.

context: who hath laid the measures thereof if thou knowest?
point_summary: God's rhetorical challenge to Job — who measured the earth's foundations? — establishes that creation's design and dimensions belong exclusively to the Creator, not to any creature who was absent at the beginning.

context: Or who hath stretched the line upon it? Whereupon are the foundations thereof fastened? Or who laid the cornerstone thereof? The morning star sang together, and all the sons of God shouted for joy.
point_summary: God's rhetorical questions to Job about measuring and founding the earth establish that He alone designed and positioned creation, a point invoked here to ground Genesis 1's account of God ordering the earth before energizing it with light.

context: though he slay me, yet I will trust him
point_summary: Job's declaration — "though he slay me, yet I will trust him" — is cited as a biblical parallel illustrating that trust in God is a deliberate, resolute decision made even in the most extreme circumstances.

context: miserable comforters are ye
point_summary: Job's label of his friends as "miserable comforters" serves as a foil, contrasting ineffective, accusatory presence with the genuinely refreshing ministry of Onesiphorus.

context: in the book of Job in particular that there are references to what we would call today dinosaurs
point_summary: Job's references to creatures like behemoth and leviathan are identified as descriptions of what we today call dinosaurs, a word not coined until the 19th century and therefore absent from the biblical text.

context: when God's speaking to Job and he speaks about Leviathan, if you read all of that and you understand
point_summary: Job's references to creatures like Leviathan describe what we would recognize as dinosaurs, though the word itself postdates the 1611 translation and should not be expected in Scripture.

context: Job 14 and verse number one man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble
point_summary: Human life is marked by trouble even for the saved, making the promise of a God who answers when called all the more precious.

context: Job asked the question, be justified with God
point_summary: Job's question — "How can a man be justified with God?" — is cited as a timeless human cry for peace with God, answered only through the gospel of peace proclaimed in Romans 10:15.

context: man that is born of woman is a few days and full of trouble
point_summary: Human life is inherently marked by trouble, as Job's words confirm, meaning peace cannot be located in trouble's absence but only in the person of Jesus Christ.
